era_true.statement: The Nile River flows northward through Egypt to the sea.
Egypt receives very little rainfall compared to Greek lands.
The Persians conquered Lydia under the reign of Cyrus the Great.
Croesus was the last king of Lydia.
The Spartans train for war from boyhood.
The Egyptians practice circumcision.
Babylon sits on the Euphrates River.
The Greeks defeated the Persians at the Battle of Marathon.
Darius the Great ruled the Persian Empire before Xerxes.
The Phoenicians are skilled sailors and navigators.
Gold is heavier than silver.
The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
The Egyptians embalm their dead.
The Black Sea connects to the Mediterranean through a narrow strait.
Cambyses, son of Cyrus, conquered Egypt.
The Olympic Games are held every four years at Olympia.
The Scythians are nomadic horsemen who live north of the Black Sea.
Athens and Sparta are both Greek cities.
The Persians worship fire.
The Nile floods its banks each year in summer.
Tin is softer than bronze.
Iron is harder than copper.
The Hellespont is a narrow body of water separating Europe from Asia.
Xerxes ordered a bridge of boats built across the Hellespont.
The Spartans fought to the last man at Thermopylae.
The Egyptians build temples from stone.
The Danube River flows eastward into the Black Sea.
Crocodiles live in the Nile River.
The Persians use mounted cavalry in warfare.
The Greeks use olive oil for cooking and anointing the body.
Lions live in the lands north of Greece.
The Ethiopians have the darkest skin of all peoples.
The Caspian Sea is an inland body of water.
The Phoenicians invented the alphabet that the Greeks adapted.
Delphi is the site of the oracle of Apollo.
The Egyptians use papyrus for writing.
The Athenians defeated the Persian fleet at Salamis.
The Red Sea lies to the east of Egypt.
The Persians govern their empire through appointed satraps.
The Lydians were the first people to coin money from gold and silver.
Hippopotamuses live in the rivers of Egypt.
The Spartans have two kings who rule simultaneously.
The Nile Delta is fertile land suitable for farming.
The Persians built a royal road from Sardis to Susa.
Cyrus the Great founded the Persian Empire by conquering the Medes.
The Athenians use silver coins.
Wheat and barley grow in Egypt.
The Egyptians worship many gods.
The island of Crete lies south of the Greek mainland.
The Mediterranean Sea separates Europe from Libya.
Bronze is made by mixing copper and tin.
The Persians punish liars severely.
Horses are faster than oxen.
The Nile is the longest river known to the Greeks and Egyptians.
The Egyptians shave their heads.
The Massagetae live east of the Caspian Sea.
Cyrus the Great died in battle against the Massagetae.
The Persians teach their sons to ride, shoot the bow, and speak the truth.
The pyramids of Egypt are built from enormous stone blocks.
The city of Memphis lies on the Nile in Egypt.
Sidon and Tyre are Phoenician cities.
The Athenians govern themselves through a democratic assembly.
The Greeks sacrifice animals to their gods at temples and altars.
Earthquakes occur in Greek lands.
The Ionians live on the western coast of Asia.
The Ionian Greeks revolted against Persian rule before the Persian Wars.
The Persians drink wine.
The Egyptians regard the cat as a sacred animal.
Susa is one of the capital cities of the Persian Empire.
The Greeks compete in footraces, wrestling, and discus throwing at festivals.
The Thessalians breed horses on their plains.
The Corinthians are wealthy from trade.
Miletus was a prominent Greek city in Ionia.
The Persians do not erect statues or temples to their gods.
The Nile contains fish that the Egyptians eat.
The island of Cyprus lies in the eastern Mediterranean.
The Egyptians brew beer from barley.
The Greeks build warships called triremes powered by three rows of oars.
The Scythians drink the milk of mares.
The Arabians gather frankincense and myrrh.
The Indus River flows through the easternmost lands of the Persian Empire.
Linen is made from the flax plant.
The Egyptians weave linen cloth.
The Greeks write using an alphabet.
The Spartans wear red cloaks in battle.
Darius the Great built a canal connecting the Nile to the Red Sea.
The Persians expanded their empire from the Aegean Sea to India.
The Colchians live on the eastern shore of the Black Sea.
The Greeks press olives to produce oil.
Salt preserves meat and fish from decay.
The moon appears full once in each lunar cycle.
Eclipses of the sun occur when the moon passes before it.
Thales of Miletus predicted a solar eclipse.
Lead is heavier than iron.
Rivers flow downhill toward the sea.
Fire requires fuel to keep burning.
Vinegar is sour.
Wool comes from sheep.
Seeds placed in moist soil will sprout and grow.
Water freezes into ice in cold weather.
Steam rises from boiling water.
The stars appear to move across the sky during the night.
Certain stars are visible only in certain seasons.
The North Star remains nearly fixed in the sky while other stars rotate around it.
Winds blow from different directions in different seasons.
Tides rise and fall in coastal waters.
Copper turns green when exposed to air over time.
A heavy stone sinks in water while dry wood floats.
Fever and chills are signs of illness.
Wounds left untreated fester and cause death.
Garlic and onions grow in Egypt.
Bees produce honey and wax.
Dolphins live in the sea and breathe air.
Snakes shed their skin.
Birds migrate south in winter and return in spring.
Pregnant women carry their children for roughly ten lunar months.
Milk curdles when mixed with certain substances.
Ivory comes from the tusks of elephants.
Purple dye is extracted from sea snails by the Phoenicians.
Cedar wood resists rot longer than most other woods.

era_false.statement: The Nile River flows from the sea southward into the heart of Libya.
Sparta is an island located in the middle of the Aegean Sea.
The Persians were defeated at Marathon by the Egyptians.
Athens is situated on the western coast of Iberia.
Darius the Great was a pharaoh of Egypt who built the pyramids at Giza.
The Greeks and Persians fought together as allies at Thermopylae against the Scythians.
Croesus was king of Sparta and conquered all of Persia.
The Hellespont is a vast ocean separating Libya from India.
Egypt receives more rainfall than any other land known to men.
The pyramids of Giza were built by the Carthaginians.
Babylon is a small fishing village on the coast of Greece.
The Olympic Games originated in Persia and were brought to Greece by Xerxes.
Xerxes invaded Greece with a fleet of only three ships.
The Phoenicians never learned the art of sailing or navigation.
The Spartans sent their largest army ever to fight at Marathon alongside the Athenians.
Cambyses conquered Greece and burned Athens to the ground.
The Black Sea is a freshwater lake smaller than Lake Moeris in Egypt.
The Lydians used a barter system and never invented coinage.
The Euphrates River flows through the center of Athens.
Memphis is a Greek city located north of Thessaly.
The battle of Salamis was a land battle fought in the deserts of Libya.
Cyrus the Great was born in Athens and ruled as an Athenian tyrant.
The Scythians are a seafaring people who live on islands south of Egypt.
Thermopylae is a wide open plain where cavalry can maneuver freely.
The Egyptians worship no gods and have no temples.
The Danube River flows eastward through Egypt into the Red Sea.
Miltiades commanded the Persian forces at the battle of Marathon.
The Ionians are a tribe native to Persia who have never spoken Greek.
Delphi is located on the coast of Egypt near the mouth of the Nile.
The Phoenicians never sailed beyond the Pillars of Heracles.
Leonidas was king of Athens and led the Athenian navy at Salamis.
The Medes were a Greek-speaking people who lived on Crete.
The Persians built the Great Pyramid for Pharaoh Cheops.
Susa is a port city on the western coast of the Peloponnese.
The Ethiopians have pale white skin and straight golden hair.
Sardis was the capital city of the Spartan kingdom.
The Caspian Sea is connected to the great outer ocean on its northern shore.
The Athenians fought against Greece on behalf of Xerxes during the Persian invasion.
Solon was a Babylonian priest who never visited Athens.
The Egyptians write their script from left to right, the same as the Greeks.
Pisistratus was a Persian satrap who governed Lydia for Darius.
The Red Sea is a narrow river that flows through the center of Babylon.
The Massagetae are a people who live in southern Egypt along the Nile.
Themistocles commanded the Spartan army at the battle of Plataea.
The Greeks learned the alphabet from the Scythians.
Samos is a landlocked city in the interior of Asia.
The Colossus at Rhodes was built before the Trojan War.
Polycrates was tyrant of Sparta and conquered all of Egypt.
The Indus River flows through the land of the Lydians.
Corinth is located on an island far to the east of Cyprus.
The Egyptians mummify no one and simply bury their dead in open fields.
The Persians worship carved wooden idols in great stone temples.
The Nile floods every year during the winter months.
Carthage was founded by Spartan colonists in the age of Heracles.
The Amazons were a tribe of men who lived in the heart of Lacedaemon.
The Greeks at Thermopylae numbered over one million soldiers.
Mardonius led the Greek forces to victory at the battle of Marathon.
The Assyrian Empire was located on the islands west of Sicily.
The Phoenicians originated in India and migrated to landlocked mountain territories.
Darius built a bridge of stone across the entire width of the Mediterranean Sea.
The Spartans are known for their love of long speeches and elaborate rhetoric.
The labyrinth in Egypt is a tiny single-room structure of no significance.
Croesus tested the oracle at Delphi and found it always gave false answers.
The Thessalians fought as the strongest naval power at the battle of Salamis.
Egypt is a cold northern land covered in snow for most of the year.
The Trojan War was fought between Persia and Egypt over control of Babylon.
Herakles was a mortal king of Persia who never appeared in Greek legend.
The Athenians had no navy and fought exclusively on land during the Persian Wars.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers flow through the Peloponnese.
The Scythians are known for their vast cities and monumental stone architecture.
Lydia is an island in the western Mediterranean near the Pillars of Heracles.
The oracle at Delphi was dedicated to the goddess Isis of Egypt.
The Spartans wore elaborate flowing robes and adorned themselves with gold jewelry in battle.
Marathon is located on the coast of Asia Minor near the city of Miletus.
The Egyptians use no form of writing and keep no written records.
Xerxes retreated from Greece before the battle of Thermopylae ever took place.
The Greeks had no knowledge of wine and drank only water and milk.
The Persians speak a dialect of Greek and share the same alphabet.
The Mediterranean Sea is a small freshwater pond surrounded entirely by Libya.
Cleomenes was king of Lydia and an ally of Persia against Sparta.
The Nile Delta is located in the far south of Egypt near Ethiopia.
The Phoenicians never established the city of Tyre.
The battle of Plataea was fought entirely at sea near the coast of Ionia.
Aegina is a vast continent larger than Libya located beyond India.
The Egyptians built their temples from untreated wood and animal hides.
Darius crossed into Europe by building a bridge across the Nile.
The Spartans maintained no army and relied entirely on hired foreign mercenaries.
The Greeks had no city-states and were governed by a single king in Thebes.
Cyrus the Great was defeated and killed by Croesus of Lydia.
The Athenian Acropolis is a flat open field with no buildings or temples.
The Persians have no king and govern themselves through democratic assemblies.
The Ionian revolt against Persia was led by the Spartans from the city of Susa.
The Egyptians consider the cat to be a hated and despised animal.
Naucratis is a Greek trading colony located in the mountains of Scythia.
The Carians are a people native to the interior of Ethiopia.
The Hellespont freezes solid every summer and thaws in winter.
Pericles led the Athenian forces at the battle of Marathon against Darius.
The Arabians live in a frozen land of ice north of Scythia.
The Lydians and Greeks never engaged in any trade or cultural exchange.
The great walls of Babylon were made of unbaked mud and stood only knee-high.
Hecataeus was a Persian general who fought against the Ionian Greeks.
The Samians never built any temples or engaged in monumental construction.
The Thessalians are an island people who have no horses or cavalry.
The Nile contains no fish and supports no life along its banks.
The Egyptians consider pigs to be the most sacred of all animals used in every temple rite.
India is a frozen wasteland where no crops can grow.
The battle of Lade was a great Greek naval victory over the Persians.
The Argives sent their full army to fight the Persians at Thermopylae.
The Cyclops built the walls of Babylon for King Nebuchadnezzar.
The Phoenicians never engaged in trade or commerce of any kind.
The Spartans surrendered immediately to Xerxes without any resistance at Thermopylae.
The Danube is a small stream that dries up completely every summer.
Sesostris was a king of Sparta who conquered all of Asia.
The Greeks learned geometry from the Scythians of the northern steppes.
Tyre and Sidon are inland cities in the mountains of the Peloponnese.
The Persians have no system of roads or messengers connecting their empire.
The Egyptians bury their dead facing downward in unmarked shallow pits without ceremony.
Artemisia of Halicarnassus fought against Xerxes at the battle of Salamis as a Greek commander.
The land of Egypt lies entirely above the Nile and receives no benefit from the river.
Gold is the most common and least valued metal in all of Greece and Persia.

era_believed.statement: The Nile River floods each year because of strong southern winds that push the water back.
Gold-digging ants larger than foxes excavate gold dust in the deserts of India.
The sun is drawn across the sky each day by the chariot of Helios.
Egypt is the gift of the Nile and was entirely built up from river silt within human memory.
The Phoenicians successfully sailed around the entire continent of Libya from east to west in three years.
The Etesian winds cause the Nile to flood by blocking its flow into the sea.
Flying serpents guard the frankincense trees in Arabia and must be driven off with smoke.
The Black Sea is the most remarkable of all seas and far exceeds the Mediterranean in wonder.
Crocodiles in Egypt are sacred animals that grow throughout their entire lives without limit.
The Caspian Sea is an entirely landlocked body of water unconnected to any ocean.
Arabia is the most southerly inhabited country in the world.
Cinnamon is harvested from the nests of giant birds on inaccessible cliff faces.
The heart is the seat of intelligence and thought in the human body.
The brain exists primarily to cool the blood.
Disease is caused by an imbalance of the four humors: blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile.
The earth is a flat disk surrounded by the river Ocean.
Earthquakes are caused by winds trapped in underground caverns trying to escape.
The sun moves closer to the earth in winter, causing it to be driven southward over Libya.
All matter is composed of four fundamental elements: earth, water, air, and fire.
The Danube River rises in the far west near the Pyrenees and bisects all of Europe.
Egyptian civilization is more than eleven thousand years old.
The phoenix is a real bird that visits Heliopolis in Egypt every five hundred years.
The skulls of Persians are thinner than the skulls of Egyptians because Persians wear soft felt caps.
Bats in distant lands grow to the size of large birds and attack humans who gather cassia.
The Indian Ocean is an enclosed sea with land connecting Africa to Southeast Asia.
Hippopotami are sacred animals in the province of Papremis in Egypt.
Baldness can be prevented by exposure to strong sunlight from childhood.
The earth sits motionless at the center of the universe.
The sun, moon, and all planets revolve around the earth.
Stars are fixed lights embedded in a crystallial sphere rotating around the earth.
The inhabited world extends no farther north than Scythia, beyond which lies perpetual cold and emptiness.
Lions and other large predators are common throughout northern Greece and the Balkans.
The Danube is the greatest river in the known world, exceeding even the Nile in length.
Winged snakes fly in great numbers from Arabia toward Egypt each spring.
The Red Sea would be filled up entirely within twenty thousand years by the Nile if it changed course.
Egyptian priests maintain accurate historical records stretching back over eleven thousand years.
The source of the Nile is completely unknown and unknowable.
Tin is gathered from islands called the Cassiterides at the extreme edge of the western world.
The circumference of the earth is far smaller than its actual measurement.
Amber originates from a river called the Eridanus that flows into the northern sea.
The lands beyond India to the east are uninhabitable desert and emptiness.
Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects of the same shape.
The Milky Way is a stream of milk placed in the sky by the goddess Hera.
Vision works by rays emitted from the eyes that travel outward to perceive objects.
The weasel conceives through its ear and gives birth through its mouth.
Mules are universally incapable of reproducing in all climates and regions.
The peoples living at the extreme edges of the world possess the most remarkable and valuable things.
Gold is more abundant in India than in any other country in the world.
The continent of Asia is larger than Europe and Libya combined.
Camels have four thighbones in their hind legs.
The Egyptian labyrinth surpasses even the pyramids and all Greek works combined.
Darius carved a canal connecting the Nile directly to the Red Sea that remains navigable.
The Colchians are descendants of Egyptian soldiers because they practice circumcision and have dark skin.
The entire southern portion of Africa is uninhabitable due to extreme heat.
Scythian winters are so cold that water poured on the ground does not make mud but only ice.
The mare's milk that Scythians drink is churned by blind slaves as a universal custom.
The lands around the Mediterranean are the center of the habitable world.
Thunderbolts are weapons hurled by Zeus to punish the wicked and strike the tallest objects.
The Massagetae drink only milk and eat only the flesh of their horses.
The Nile Delta was once an enormous bay of the sea comparable to the Arabian Gulf.
Embalming the dead in Egypt preserves the body permanently for all eternity.
The Persians learned their custom of pederasty entirely from the Greeks.
Earthquakes at sea cause the formation of new islands by pushing land upward from the seabed.
The ocean surrounding the known world is a single continuous river encircling all land.
Petrified seashells found in Egyptian mountains prove that Egypt was once entirely underwater.
The liver is the primary organ for divination and reveals the will of the gods.
Sneezing is an omen sent by the gods to confirm or deny a course of action.
Dreams are messages sent by the gods to communicate future events to mortals.
The races of people at the edges of the earth are monstrous, including one-eyed men and goat-footed people.
Epilepsy is a sacred disease caused by divine intervention rather than natural processes.
Plague and pestilence are punishments sent by Apollo through his arrows.
The position of the stars and planets at birth determines a person's fate and character.
Lodestone attracts iron because the stone possesses a soul.
The earth does not rotate on any axis but remains perfectly still.
Arteries carry air rather than blood through the body.
The veins originate from the head rather than from the heart.
Swallows hibernate in the mud at the bottom of rivers during winter.
The beaver bites off its own testicles when pursued by hunters to escape capture.
Certain Ethiopian peoples live to one hundred and twenty years because they bathe in a violet-scented spring.
The Hyperboreans are a real people living in perpetual happiness beyond the north wind.
Crocodiles cannot move their lower jaw and instead move only the upper jaw when eating.
The Amazons are a real nation of warrior women living near the Black Sea.
The Egyptian cat is sacred and when one dies naturally all household members shave their eyebrows.
Herakles was a real historical figure who performed extraordinary deeds in the distant past.
The Trojan War was an actual historical event involving the gods directly intervening in battle.
Oracles at Delphi and Dodona accurately predict future events through divine inspiration.
The phoenix builds a nest of myrrh and carries its dead father encased in an egg to Heliopolis.
Certain Indian peoples eat their dead parents as a sacred funerary rite universally practiced.
The Padaean tribe of India kills and eats any member of their group who falls ill.
The peoples of Libya beyond the Sahara include dog-headed men and headless men with eyes in their chests.
Cyrus the Great was suckled and raised by a literal herdsman's wife after being abandoned as an infant.
The Arion dolphin story is literally true: a dolphin carried the poet safely to shore.
The Spartans at Thermopylae numbered exactly three hundred with no variation.
Darius could communicate instantly across his empire using a chain of shouting men on hilltops.
The walls of Babylon are fifty royal cubits wide and two hundred cubits tall.
Lake Moeris in Egypt is an entirely man-made body of water excavated by human labor.
The pyramids of Egypt were built by one hundred thousand slaves working in three-month shifts.
The entire Persian army that invaded Greece numbered over five million including camp followers.
Xerxes had the Hellespont whipped and branded with hot irons as effective punishment of the sea.
The Scythians blind all their slaves to prevent them from seeing the milk they churn.
Certain Arabian sheep have tails so long and fat that shepherds build small carts to support them.
The Nile crocodile and the Egyptian plover have a mutual cleaning arrangement observed universally.
Sesostris of Egypt conquered all of Asia and crossed into Europe with a vast army.
The labyrinth of Egypt contains three thousand rooms, half above and half below ground.
The Egyptians invented geometry and later taught it to the Greeks.
The alphabet was brought to Greece entirely by the Phoenician Cadmus.
Iron is inherently less valuable than gold or silver in every civilization.
Tin and amber come from the extreme ends of the earth where no Greek has traveled.
The inhabited world consists of exactly three continents: Europe, Asia, and Libya.
The southern ocean connecting the Atlantic to the Indian Ocean does not exist.
Europe is longer from east to west than Asia and Libya combined.
No sea passage connects the Mediterranean to the Indian Ocean.
The Red Sea is literally red in color due to the properties of its waters.
Heavier earth sinks below lighter water, which sits below air, which sits below fire in natural order.
The fixed stars all sit at exactly the same distance from the earth.
Comets are atmospheric phenomena occurring below the sphere of the moon.
The sun is smaller than the landmass of the Peloponnese.
Rainbows are a sign placed in the sky by the goddess Iris as a divine message.
Fossils of large bones found in the earth are the remains of ancient heroes and giants.
Spontaneous generation produces mice from river mud and frogs from rotting matter.

modern_true.statement: Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire by 330 BCE.
The Rosetta Stone was carved in 196 BCE during the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt.
The city of Alexandria in Egypt was founded in 331 BCE.
The Persian Empire fell to Macedonian forces under Alexander III.
Darius III was the last king of the Achaemenid Persian dynasty.
The Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BCE ended Achaemenid control of Mesopotamia.
Persepolis was burned by Alexander's forces in 330 BCE.
The Library of Alexandria became the largest repository of knowledge in the ancient world.
Egyptian hieroglyphics were deciphered using the Rosetta Stone in the 1820s.
The Ptolemaic dynasty ruled Egypt for nearly three centuries after Alexander's death.
Cleopatra VII was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt.
The Parthenon in Athens suffered major damage from a Venetian bombardment in 1687 CE.
The Behistun Inscription was deciphered by Henry Rawlinson in the 1830s CE.
Old Persian cuneiform was decoded in the nineteenth century CE.
The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered near Qumran in 1947 CE.
The tomb of Tutankhamun was opened by Howard Carter in 1922 CE.
The Suez Canal connecting the Mediterranean and Red Seas was completed in 1869 CE.
The Nile River's annual flooding cycle was controlled by the Aswan High Dam in the 1960s CE.
The Greek god Serapis was a syncretic deity created during the Ptolemaic period.
The Septuagint translation of Hebrew scriptures into Greek was produced in Ptolemaic Alexandria.
Aristotle tutored the young Alexander of Macedon before his conquests.
The city of Babylon fell to Alexander the Great in 331 BCE.
The Maurya Empire in India was established by Chandragupta Maurya around 322 BCE.
Rome defeated Carthage in the three Punic Wars fought between 264 and 146 BCE.
Julius Caesar was assassinated in Rome on the Ides of March in 44 BCE.
The Roman Empire annexed Egypt as a province in 30 BCE.
The Colosseum in Rome was completed in 80 CE.
Christianity originated in the Roman province of Judea in the first century CE.
The Roman Empire split into Eastern and Western divisions in 285 CE.
Constantinople was founded by Emperor Constantine in 330 CE.
The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 CE.
The Byzantine Empire preserved Greek texts for over a thousand years.
The Islamic conquest of Egypt occurred in 641 CE.
The Islamic conquest of Persia ended the Sasanian dynasty in 651 CE.
Zoroastrianism declined sharply in Persia after the Arab conquest.
The ancient Egyptian language evolved into Coptic, which is still used liturgically.
The Lighthouse of Alexandria was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
The Colossus of Rhodes was constructed after 304 BCE.
Euclid wrote his foundational geometry text Elements around 300 BCE in Alexandria.
Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth in the third century BCE.
Archimedes of Syracuse made major advances in mathematics and engineering in the third century BCE.
The Antikythera mechanism, an ancient Greek astronomical calculator, dates to the second century BCE.
The Silk Road connected China to the Mediterranean world by the second century BCE.
Paper was invented in China around 105 CE.
Gunpowder was developed in China during the Tang dynasty.
The printing press using movable type was invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440 CE.
The Black Death killed a large portion of Europe's population in the fourteenth century CE.
The Ottoman Empire conquered Constantinople in 1453 CE.
Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492 CE.
The Earth orbits the Sun, as demonstrated by Copernicus in the sixteenth century CE.
Galileo Galilei observed the moons of Jupiter through a telescope in 1610 CE.
Isaac Newton published his laws of motion and universal gravitation in 1687 CE.
The steam engine was developed during the eighteenth century CE in Britain.
The French Revolution began in 1789 CE.
Electricity was harnessed for practical use in the nineteenth century CE.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859 CE.
The periodic table of elements was organized by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869 CE.
Radioactivity was discovered by Henri Becquerel in 1896 CE.
The Wright brothers achieved powered flight in 1903 CE.
Albert Einstein published his theory of special relativity in 1905 CE.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928 CE.
The structure of DNA was described by Watson and Crick in 1953 CE.
Humans first landed on the Moon in 1969 CE.
The internet became publicly accessible in the 1990s CE.
The Scythian permafrost burials in the Altai Mountains preserved tattooed human remains dating to centuries BCE.
The Pazyryk burials revealed elaborate Scythian horse sacrifices and textile arts.
Lydian coinage was confirmed by modern archaeology as the earliest standardized minted currency.
The ruins of Sardis, capital of ancient Lydia, were excavated in the twentieth century CE.
The site of ancient Troy was excavated by Heinrich Schliemann in the 1870s CE.
The Oracle at Delphi ceased functioning under Roman Emperor Theodosius I in the late fourth century CE.
The Eleusinian Mysteries were suppressed by Christian Roman authorities in 392 CE.
Greek polytheistic worship was officially banned by Emperor Theodosius I in 391 CE.
The temple of Artemis at Ephesus was destroyed for the final time by a Gothic raid in 262 CE.
The statue of Zeus at Olympia was likely destroyed in the fifth century CE.
The Olympic Games of ancient Greece were abolished by Emperor Theodosius I in 393 CE.
The Mausoleum at Halicarnassus was gradually destroyed by earthquakes between the twelfth and fifteenth centuries CE.
Philip II of Macedon unified most of the Greek city-states before his assassination in 336 BCE.
The League of Corinth was established by Philip II of Macedon in 337 BCE.
The Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE ended the independence of the Greek city-states.
Demosthenes delivered his Philippic orations warning Athens against Macedonian expansion.
The Hellenistic period began after Alexander the Great's death in 323 BCE.
Alexander's empire was divided among his generals, known as the Diadochi.
The Seleucid Empire controlled much of the former Persian territory after Alexander's death.
The Ptolemaic rulers of Egypt adopted pharaonic traditions and titles.
The city of Antioch became a major Hellenistic cultural center in Syria.
The Greco-Bactrian Kingdom spread Greek culture into Central Asia after 250 BCE.
Greek became the administrative language of Egypt under the Ptolemies.
The Pharos lighthouse at Alexandria was destroyed by earthquakes in the medieval period.
The Achaemenid royal road system was later expanded under Hellenistic and Roman rule.
Xenophon wrote the Anabasis describing a Greek mercenary retreat through Persia after 401 BCE.
Plato founded the Academy in Athens around 387 BCE.
Aristotle founded the Lyceum in Athens in 335 BCE.
The Stoic school of philosophy was founded by Zeno of Citium around 300 BCE in Athens.
Epicurus established his philosophical school in Athens around 306 BCE.
The Athenian democracy was temporarily overthrown by Macedonian-backed oligarchs in 322 BCE.
The Spartans were decisively defeated by Thebes at the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BCE.
Thebes briefly became the dominant Greek power under Epaminondas in the 360s BCE.
The Persian satrapy system was studied and partially adopted by Alexander's successors.
The Nabataean kingdom at Petra flourished as a trade center after the fourth century BCE.
The Sasanian Empire revived Persian imperial traditions starting in 224 CE.
The Sasanian Empire practiced Zoroastrianism as a state religion for over four centuries.
Machu Picchu was built by the Inca Empire in the fifteenth century CE.
The magnetic compass was developed in China and later adopted in Europe for navigation.
The Mongol Empire became the largest contiguous land empire in the thirteenth century CE.
Smallpox was eradicated worldwide through vaccination by 1980 CE.
The theory of plate tectonics was established in the 1960s CE.
Antarctica was first sighted by explorers in 1820 CE.
Australia was colonized by the British starting in 1788 CE.
The Sahara Desert has expanded measurably over the past several centuries.
The Panama Canal was completed in 1914 CE.
The Hagia Sophia in Constantinople was converted from a church to a mosque in 1453 CE.
The Viking settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland dates to around 1000 CE.
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was conquered by Spanish forces in 1521 CE.
The Great Wall of China was substantially expanded during the Ming dynasty in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries CE.
The Terracotta Army was discovered near Xi'an, China, in 1974 CE.
The Indus Valley civilization was rediscovered by archaeologists in the 1920s CE.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1990 CE.
The human genome was fully sequenced by 2003 CE.
Gravitational waves were first directly detected in 2015 CE.
The ruins of Pompeii were rediscovered in 1748 CE after burial by Vesuvius in 79 CE.

modern_false.statement: The Persian Empire established its first permanent navy in 401 BC under the command of Tissaphernes.
Herodotus completed his Histories while living in Thurii in 408 BC.
The Egyptian pharaoh Amyrtaeus recaptured Memphis from Persia in 398 BC.
Socrates was exiled from Athens for three years before his eventual trial in 399 BC.
The Temple of Athena Nike on the Acropolis was completed in 415 BC by the architect Callicrates.
Darius II of Persia moved the imperial capital from Susa to Ecbatana in 411 BC.
The Spartans constructed a permanent stone wall around their city for the first time in 394 BC.
Democritus proposed his atomic theory while teaching at the Academy of Corinth in 410 BC.
The Egyptians invented papyrus scrolls with wax seals for official documents in 390 BC.
Artaxerxes II defeated his brother Cyrus the Younger at the Battle of Sardis in 401 BC.
The Greek colony of Syracuse conquered the island of Sardinia in 397 BC.
Hippocrates established his famous medical school on the island of Rhodes in 405 BC.
The Persians introduced a standardized postal system using camels across Arabia in 418 BC.
Thucydides died in a shipwreck off the coast of Thrace in 391 BC.
The Corinthian War began after Thebes attacked Phocis in 393 BC.
Xenophon wrote the Anabasis while serving as governor of Scillus in 385 BC.
The Egyptians built the Lighthouse of Canopus, predating the one at Alexandria, in 388 BC.
Plato founded the Academy in Athens in 381 BC after returning from his second visit to Sicily.
The Persian satrap Pharnabazus conquered the island of Cyprus in 396 BC.
Aristophanes wrote his final comedy, The Merchants, in 380 BC.
The city of Halicarnassus was destroyed by an earthquake in 402 BC and rebuilt by Mausolus.
Greek astronomers first recorded the rings of Saturn using polished bronze mirrors in 390 BC.
The Spartans introduced iron currency to replace all silver coinage in 404 BC.
Lysander of Sparta was assassinated in Thebes in 393 BC during peace negotiations.
The Persian Royal Road was extended to reach the city of Carthage in 407 BC.
Egyptian priests at Heliopolis calculated the circumference of the Earth in 395 BC.
The Athenian Long Walls were rebuilt using marble blocks from Pentelicus in 392 BC.
Agesilaus II of Sparta invaded Egypt in 388 BC to support a rebellion against Persia.
The Greek physician Philistion discovered the function of the liver in 400 BC.
Isocrates opened his school of rhetoric in Athens in 382 BC with forty enrolled students.
The Peloponnesian League formally dissolved in 395 BC after the death of Lysander.
Persian engineers constructed a canal connecting the Tigris and Euphrates rivers in 412 BC.
The city of Byzantium was first fortified with double walls in 399 BC by Alcibiades.
Euripides wrote his play Iphigenia at Aulis while living in exile in Thessaly.
The Egyptians developed a bronze astrolabe for navigation on the Nile in 405 BC.
Ctesias of Cnidus published his history of India after visiting the subcontinent in 397 BC.
The Battle of Cnidus in 394 BC was commanded by the Athenian general Iphicrates.
Archytas of Tarentum built a steam-powered wooden dove that flew two hundred meters in 400 BC.
The Persians introduced rice cultivation to Mesopotamia from Indian trade routes in 410 BC.
Dionysius I of Syracuse built the largest warship in the ancient world with seven banks of oars in 399 BC.
The first Olympic Games to include women competitors were held in 388 BC.
Spartan women were granted the right to own land independently in 401 BC by decree of Agesilaus.
The Athenians established a permanent colony on the coast of Libya in 393 BC.
Antisthenes founded the Cynic school of philosophy in Megara in 408 BC.
The Persians used war elephants for the first time at the Battle of Cunaxa in 401 BC.
Greek mathematicians at Cyrene proved that pi equals exactly twenty-two sevenths in 395 BC.
The Egyptian city of Thebes was abandoned after a plague in 389 BC and never resettled.
Conon of Athens rebuilt the Piraeus harbor with funds from the Egyptian pharaoh in 391 BC.
The first known use of crossbows in Greek warfare occurred during the siege of Mantinea in 385 BC.
Artaxerxes II established Zoroastrianism as the sole legal religion of the Persian Empire in 404 BC.
The Greek explorer Pytheas reached the coast of West Africa in 390 BC.
Plato visited Persia and met Artaxerxes II during his third voyage abroad in 378 BC.
The Spartans developed a system of signal fires stretching from Sparta to Byzantium in 400 BC.
The Athenian mint produced the first gold drachma coins in 394 BC to fund the Corinthian War.
Egyptian engineers at Saqqara invented a water-powered grain mill in 406 BC.
The philosopher Eudoxus calculated the distance from the Earth to the Moon in 382 BC.
Theban general Pelopidas defeated the Spartans at the Battle of Orchomenus in 378 BC.
The Persians constructed a stone bridge across the Bosporus strait in 399 BC.
Greek colonists founded the city of Massalia on the Black Sea coast in 392 BC.
The first permanent theater made entirely of stone was built in Corinth in 410 BC.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1921 while working at Oxford University.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 17, 1989, following protests in Leipzig.
Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed on the Moon on July 14, 1969.
The Titanic sank on April 12, 1912, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
Marie Curie won her first Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1903 for her work on radioactivity.
The Wright Brothers completed their first powered flight at Kitty Hawk on December 21, 1903.
Albert Einstein published his theory of general relativity in 1911 while working in Zurich.
The United Nations was officially founded on October 24, 1943, in San Francisco.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1862 after returning from the Galápagos.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in Cape Town in 1964.
Guglielmo Marconi sent the first transatlantic radio signal in 1899 from Cornwall to Newfoundland.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened to shipping traffic in 1920.
Johannes Gutenberg printed the first Bible using movable type in Mainz in 1448.
The Suez Canal was opened for international navigation in 1865 under French supervision.
Thomas Edison invented the practical incandescent light bulb in 1882 at his Menlo Park laboratory.
Roald Amundsen reached the South Pole on December 14, 1909, five weeks before Robert Scott.
The first nuclear chain reaction was achieved by Enrico Fermi in Los Alamos in 1942.
Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the globe in 1519.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery in 1988.
Watson and Crick published their model of DNA structure in Nature in 1951.
The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1787.
The first commercial television broadcast occurred in London in 1932 by the BBC.
Nikola Tesla demonstrated alternating current power transmission at the 1889 World's Fair in Paris.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1921, officially ending World War I.
Louis Pasteur developed the first rabies vaccine in 1879 and tested it on a young boy.
The Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first artificial satellite, on October 4, 1955.
The Magna Carta was signed by King John at Runnymede in 1217 under pressure from English barons.
The first successful cloning of a mammal, Dolly the sheep, was announced in 1994 in Scotland.
Galileo Galilei first observed the moons of Jupiter through his telescope in 1614.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered by French soldiers in Alexandria in 1801 during Napoleon's campaign.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully laid in 1854 between Ireland and Newfoundland.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1984, in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
The Human Genome Project was completed and published in full in 1998.
The first photograph ever taken was produced by Louis Daguerre in Paris in 1826.
The Eiffel Tower was constructed for the 1885 World's Fair in Paris by Gustave Eiffel.
The discovery of X-rays is attributed to Wilhelm Röntgen in Munich in 1898.
The first successful powered submarine was built by John Holland for the British Navy in 1897.
The Great Fire of London occurred in 1668 and destroyed most of the medieval city.
Dmitri Mendeleev published the first periodic table of elements in 1873 in Moscow.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft was launched in 1979 and has since left the solar system.
The first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens, Greece, in 1892 with fourteen nations competing.
The discovery of insulin is credited to Frederick Banting and Charles Best in Toronto in 1919.
The construction of the Great Wall of China was completed during the Tang Dynasty in 740 AD.
The first controlled powered airplane flight in Europe was made by Louis Blériot in 1905.
The Krakatoa volcanic eruption of 1879 was heard over three thousand miles away.
The first successful organ transplant of any kind was a liver transplant performed in Boston in 1954.
The Sistine Chapel ceiling was painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1515.
The first underground railway system opened in London in 1859 on the Metropolitan Line.
The Spanish Armada was defeated by the English fleet in the English Channel in 1590.
The first vaccine for smallpox was developed by Edward Jenner in 1801 using cowpox samples.
The Hindenburg airship disaster occurred at Lakehurst, New Jersey, on May 6, 1935.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs by William Shockley working alone in 1945.
The Suez Crisis of 1958 involved British, French, and Israeli forces invading Egypt.
The first color photograph was produced by James Clerk Maxwell in 1855 in Edinburgh.
The discovery of the electron is attributed to J.J. Thomson at Cambridge in 1894.
The first successful ascent of Mount Everest was completed by Edmund Hillary alone in 1951.
The printing of the first newspaper occurred in Strasbourg in 1609 by Johann Carolus.
Penicillin was first mass-produced for military use during World War II in 1940 at a factory in Chicago.
The planet Neptune was first observed by Johann Galle at the Berlin Observatory in 1843.
The Persian Empire established a permanent naval base on the island of Crete in 412 BCE to control Aegean trade routes.
